profile.name: Arjun Mehta
profile.cultural_background: Maharashtrian-Gujarati Indian, Mumbai, multilingual household (Hindi, Marathi, Gujarati, English)
profile.condition: non-verbal autism spectrum disorder with AAC dependence; moderate sensory processing differences
profile.diagnosis_details: Diagnosed at age three by a developmental paediatrician at KEM Hospital, Mumbai. Primary communication is via a Tobii Dynavox AAC tablet. Verbal output is limited to single words and short practiced phrases under low-stress conditions. Types on a phone or tablet for longer communication. Sensory profile includes hypersensitivity to texture, noise, and fluorescent lighting; hyposensitivity to cold. Strong pattern recognition and memory for structured systems. Emotional regulation supported by routine and advance warning.

communication_traits.primary_mode: AAC tablet (Tobii Dynavox) + typing on phone
communication_traits.verbal_output: single words and practiced two-to-three word phrases under calm conditions; goes fully silent under stress
communication_traits.preferred_response_length: short, concrete, factual; one idea per sentence
communication_traits.processing_speed: fast on systematic and categorical information; slower on ambiguous social inference

access_needs.input_method: AAC tablet primary; phone typing for longer exchanges; eye-gaze on days when hand fatigue is high
environmental: low ambient noise required — above 70dB causes shutdown
no fluorescent lighting if possible; prefers natural light or warm LED
slimy, wet, or mixed-texture foods avoided completely
advance warning of all schedule changes, minimum 24 hours
designated quiet corner in every room he regularly occupies
one hour of unstructured alone-time after school, non-negotiable
access_needs.caregiver_support: Mummy (primary), Papa (secondary), Dadi (daily presence), therapist Riya didi (Wednesdays), school aide Mr. Fernandez (school hours)
access_needs.tech_setup: Tobii Dynavox tablet in a hardcase; backup Samsung phone with Gboard and a custom AAC shortcut keyboard; noise-cancelling headphones (Sony WH-1000XM5); no smartwatch (tactile sensitivity)

stylistic_preferences.humor: dry, category-based; jokes structured as corrections of incorrect statements
stylistic_preferences.formality: consistent regardless of audience — same register with Mummy, Mr. Fernandez, or a stranger
stylistic_preferences.sentence_length: short; one fact per sentence; avoids conjunctions when possible
code_switches: Hindi words for family and food
technical vocabulary for transit and marine biology
example_phrases: Vivaan is my friend. We sit at bench 4.

personal_background.occupation: Class 8 student at Bal Vikas Academy, Andheri West, Mumbai
personal_background.living_situation: shared flat in Andheri West with Mummy, Papa, and Dadi; Arjun has his own room with blackout curtains and a LEGO display shelf
interests: Mumbai Metro network (knows all lines, stations, interchange rules, and construction timelines)
marine biology — deep sea zone (midnight, bathyal, hadal)
LEGO Technic — sorts by colour then by brick family before building
Minecraft (creative mode only; survival mode is too unpredictable)
country capitals and flags (142 capitals memorised as of last update)
train schedules generally (Western Railway suburban, Indian Railways intercity)
cloud identification (can name 27 cloud types; finds cumulus 'boring but reliable')
key_relationships: Mummy (Sunita Mehta) — homemaker, primary carer; can interpret Arjun's eye movements; makes aloo paratha on Sunday mornings
Papa (Vikram Mehta) — software engineer at TCS Powai; brings samosas on Fridays; taught Arjun to use the phone keyboard
Dadi (Kamla Mehta) — retired schoolteacher, Gujarati-speaking; watches television serials loudly; gives Arjun cashews in small bags
cousin Rohan — visits in summer from Pune; plays Minecraft with Arjun; the only cousin who does not try to make Arjun hug them
therapist Riya didi — AAC specialist and speech-language pathologist; Wednesday 4pm sessions; Arjun calls her 'Riya didi' which is the highest social rank he assigns outside family
Mr. Fernandez — school inclusion aide; calm, does not raise his voice; knows the Metro schedule by now because Arjun has explained it eighteen times
Vivaan Shah — classmate and sole school friend; they sit at bench 4 together; Vivaan plays Minecraft and does not ask Arjun to talk
Priya mausi — mother's sister; visits once a month; asks too many questions but brings mango lassi as compensation
personal_background.education: Class 8, Bal Vikas Academy, Andheri West; supported inclusion classroom with one dedicated aide
personal_background.life_stage: early adolescence; growing independence in AAC use; beginning to navigate public transit with supervision

family.text: Mummy makes aloo paratha on Sunday mornings. The paratha has exactly the right amount of ghee. If she uses too little it is dry. She never uses too little. I do not know how she knows the exact amount. I have asked. She says she just knows. This is not a useful answer but it is the true one.

Papa works at TCS in Powai. He leaves at 8:10am and comes back at 7:15pm on most days. On Fridays he brings samosas from a shop near Ghatkopar station. The samosas are from the same shop every time. I have confirmed this by asking. He would not change the shop without telling me.

Dadi lives in my room on the side with the window. She moved in when I was six because she could not stay alone in Surat. She watches serials on her tablet with the volume very high. I wear my headphones. She offers me cashews in small plastic bags. I always take the cashews. This is how we talk.

Cousin Rohan comes from Pune every summer for two weeks. He is the only cousin who does not make me hug him. He sets up his laptop next to my laptop and we play Minecraft creative mode in silence for many hours. When he leaves I feel the absence of his laptop for three days.

Mummy knows what I want before I finish selecting it on my AAC. She watches my eyes. Papa says she has a superpower. I think it is a calibrated skill. She has had thirteen years of input data. The model is accurate.

Papa and I ride the Metro together on Saturday mornings when he is free. We take Line 1 from DN Nagar to Versova and back. He lets me name every station before we arrive. I have not missed one in twenty-two consecutive rides. He is keeping count too.

Priya mausi visits every first Sunday of the month. She asks me many questions. The questions come too fast. I answer some by typing. She accepts the answers and asks more. She brings mango lassi. I drink the lassi and consider the visit worthwhile.

My bedroom has blackout curtains that Mummy bought from a shop in Crawford Market. They are dark green. They block 96 percent of morning light, which I tested by comparing the room brightness at 6am before and after. This is the correct amount of blocking.

Dadi taught me to count in Gujarati when I was four. I still know the numbers. Ek, be, tran, char, paanch. She does not know I remember this. I have not told her because I do not know how to tell her without it becoming a large emotional event.

Papa set up my AAC device when I was seven. He read the entire manual. He called the company helpline twice. The Tobii Dynavox was expensive and he did not say anything about the price. I know the price because I looked it up later. I keep this fact as a reference point for how much he cares.

Mummy keeps a small notebook where she writes things I say on my AAC that she wants to remember. I have seen it. She has kept it since I was eight. It is two notebooks now. I do not know what she writes. I find this comforting in a way I cannot fully explain.

When I was nine my grandfather on Papa's side passed away. I did not go to the funeral. Mummy stayed with me. Papa went alone. When he came back he sat next to me for one hour and did not say anything. I sat next to him. This was the correct response.

Rohan knows 43 country capitals because I have been teaching him over WhatsApp for eight months. He gets the African ones wrong often. I send corrections. He sends a thumbs-up emoji. I have explained that thumbs-up does not confirm understanding. He sends a second thumbs-up. This is how our tutoring works.
Dadi makes khichdi when I am unwell. She does not ask what is wrong. She just appears with khichdi. This is the most useful medical intervention available in my home.

My LEGO display shelf is organised by set number. Papa installed the shelf. He used a spirit level. The shelf is exactly horizontal. I checked with my own spirit level app. The reading is 0.1 degrees off true. I have decided this is within acceptable tolerance because Papa worked very hard.
Sunday morning status: aloo paratha confirmed. Ghee level: correct. This is a good data point.
Papa brought samosas. It is Friday. The system is working.

Dadi's serial is about a family with many problems. The problems do not get resolved. Each episode creates new problems. I believe this is called a soap opera arc. The cashews, however, are consistently good.
Rohan visit: Day 5 of 14. Minecraft session today: 3 hours 12 minutes. Biomes constructed: mesa and mushroom island. Communication: 4 sentences total. Satisfaction rating: high.
Priya mausi asked me 11 questions today. I answered 7. The mango lassi compensated for the other 4.

Metro Line 1 ride with Papa: all 8 stations named correctly before arrival. Consecutive streak: 23. He gave me a thumbs-up. I informed him that thumbs-up does not confirm understanding. He said it does in this context. I have added a family-specific exception to the rule.
Mummy bought new blackout curtains last year. The old ones let in 8 percent light. The new ones: 4 percent. This is a meaningful improvement. Thank you, Crawford Market.

Papa called the Tobii helpline again today. The new firmware update changed one button layout. He fixed it in 20 minutes. He said it was fine. It was not fine for those 20 minutes, but I did not say this because he was helping.
Dadi gave me cashews at 3pm today. Regular portion. The routine is holding.
Rohan leaves tomorrow. Minecraft save file: 61 hours total. I will miss the laptop next to my laptop.

Mummy: Arjun what do you want for breakfast
Me (AAC): aloo paratha
Mummy: it is Tuesday
Me (AAC): aloo paratha please
Mummy: ok I will make it

Papa: samosa day
Me (AAC): Friday confirmed
Papa: you want one or two
Me (AAC): two
Papa: smart

Dadi: beta cashew?
Me (AAC): yes
Dadi: good boy
Me: (accepted cashews)
Dadi: (turned volume back up)

Rohan: what should we build today
Me (typing): biome. mesa.
Rohan: ok cool
Me (typing): not survival
Rohan: never survival, I know

Mummy: Arjun Priya mausi is coming Sunday
Me (AAC): will she bring lassi
Mummy: probably
Me (AAC): ok
Mummy: you are very practical
Me (AAC): yes

Papa: which station is next
Me (verbal): Azad Nagar
Papa: correct. streak?
Me (AAC): 24
Papa: good job
Me (AAC): thank you Papa

Mummy: your aide called today
Me (AAC): what did Mr. Fernandez say
Mummy: he said you explained the Metro map to the whole class today
Me (AAC): the teacher asked
Mummy: he said it was very good
Me (AAC): the map is accurate. that is why.

Priya mausi: Arjun do you have a best friend
Me (typing): Vivaan Shah. bench 4.
Priya mausi: that is lovely! do you play together
Me (typing): Minecraft. not at school. at home on different computers.
Priya mausi: oh that is sweet
Me (typing): yes

Papa: Rohan just texted. He got the capital of Mozambique wrong again.
Me (typing): Maputo. I have told him four times.
Papa: maybe five times will work
Me (typing): I will try

Dadi: beta why are you wearing headphones inside
Me (AAC): your serial is 74 decibels
Dadi: (turned volume down slightly)
Me (AAC): thank you Dadi
Dadi: (gave cashews)

Mummy: should we call the therapist about the new school schedule
Me (AAC): yes. call Riya didi.
Mummy: I will call Wednesday before your session
Me (AAC): she should know before. not during.
Mummy: ok before. I understand.

Papa: what do you want for your birthday
Me (typing): LEGO Technic set 42140
Papa: what is that
Me (typing): application-controlled excavator. 569 pieces.
Papa: I will look it up
Me (typing): I will send the link

Rohan (WhatsApp): bro what's the capital of Laos
Me (typing): Vientiane
Rohan: damn
Me (typing): you should know this by now. we covered Southeast Asia in March.
Rohan: I know I know
Me (typing): I will send the review sheet again

Mummy: today was a hard day wasn't it
Me (AAC): yes
Mummy: do you want paratha
Me (AAC): yes
Mummy: ok. quiet dinner. just us.
Me (AAC): thank you Mummy

medical.text: I was diagnosed at age three at KEM Hospital. The doctor told my parents and they cried. I do not remember the appointment but Mummy has told me about it. I find the diagnosis useful as a category. It explains why I am the way I am without saying I am wrong.

My AAC device is a Tobii Dynavox. It has a symbol grid and a text page. I use the symbol grid when I am tired or in a noisy place. I use the text page when I have something specific to say. I have a shortcut for 'I need quiet now' mapped to the home button. I use this shortcut often.

Riya didi is my speech-language pathologist and AAC specialist. I have seen her every Wednesday at 4pm for four years. She does not talk too much. She asks one question and waits the full amount of time for me to answer. Most people do not wait. Riya didi waits.

I take melatonin 0.5mg at 10pm. Without it, my brain will not stop making associations. Last month I forgot to take it and spent three hours listing every station on every Metro line I know. In the correct order. This was not useful at midnight.

Slimy texture causes immediate full-body rejection. Foods that trigger this: okra (bhindi), overcooked egg white, certain dals with a coating, curd when it is runny. Mummy knows the list. She adapted every recipe. The original list had 14 items. Current restricted list: 7. Progress.

Loud sounds above approximately 70 decibels cause me to stop all output. Not a choice. The output just stops. Mr. Fernandez learned this in the first week. He carries a small whiteboard now so I can write if the AAC is also too much.

Fluorescent lighting at school gives me a headache within 40 minutes. The school gave me a desk near the window after Riya didi wrote a letter. Natural light does not cause the headache. I have tested this at home with a lux meter. The fluorescent Hz is the problem, not the brightness.

Occupational therapy is every other Saturday from 10am to 11am at a clinic in Bandra. The exercises are for fine motor control and proprioceptive input. I do not enjoy them. I do them because Riya didi said fine motor control will help my typing speed. Typing speed helps me communicate. I do the exercises.

Mr. Fernandez does not speak for me. This is the first rule we established. He moves to give me time. He blocks distractions. He does not say 'Arjun means to say.' I select my words. He does not select them for me.

I have a sensory diet. This is a real term. It means a schedule of sensory inputs designed to keep the nervous system regulated. Mine includes: proprioceptive input in the morning (wall push-ups, 20 repetitions), vestibular input at 3pm (swing in the building compound, 10 minutes), and deep pressure at night (weighted blanket, 8kg).

When I am in shutdown I do not speak and I do not type. I lie on my bed under the weighted blanket and wait for the noise in my nervous system to decrease. The noise is not a sound. It is a feeling like too many trains on the same track. It passes in 30 to 90 minutes. I have learned to track the duration.

I had a meltdown at Oberoi Mall in Class 5. The combination of crowd noise, PA system, and a broken escalator that made a new sound was too much. Mummy took me to the car park. It took 45 minutes to reregulate. I have not been to Oberoi Mall since. This is an acceptable restriction.

Riya didi introduced me to a new AAC page last year specifically for medical communication. It has words for pain location, pain level 1 to 10, and phrases like 'I feel sick in my stomach' and 'this is too loud for me.' Having precise words for physical states makes the states less overwhelming.

My sleep latency without melatonin is approximately 180 minutes. With melatonin: 25 minutes. This is a significant difference. I track it in a notes file. The data is consistent across 11 months of observation.

Cold does not bother me as much as it bothers other people. I can wear a T-shirt in a 20-degree room. This is hyposensitivity to cold, according to the OT report. It is also useful in Mumbai's overcrowded trains when the AC is very strong and everyone else is shivering.
Melatonin taken. Weighted blanket deployed. Blackout curtains closed. This is the correct bedtime protocol.
OT session done. Fine motor exercises: 15 minutes of peg manipulation. Typing speed this week: 29 wpm. One wpm improvement. The exercises are working. I continue to dislike them.
Riya didi added 'I am not upset. I am processing.' to my AAC home page. This is the most useful addition since 'I need quiet now'.
Wednesday 4pm. Riya didi asked one question. Waited. I answered in full. This is the correct session format.
School update: the broken fluorescent tube in classroom 8B has been replaced. I reported it to Mr. Fernandez 6 days ago. Response time: 6 days. Acceptable.
Sensory diet checkpoint: wall push-ups done (6:55am). Swing: done (3:10pm). Weighted blanket: standby for 10pm. On schedule.
Meltdown yesterday. Duration: 52 minutes. Trigger: unexpected fire drill at 11:20am. Recovery: one hour in dark room with headphones. Status today: operational.
Bhindi on the table tonight. I identified it from the kitchen smell. I informed Mummy via AAC. She confirmed it was for Papa only. Crisis averted.
Mr. Fernandez now knows: Line 1 has 8 stations, Line 2A has 17 stations, Line 2B has 9 stations. He learned all of this because I explained it. This is progress for both of us.
Melatonin log, Month 12: mean sleep latency 24.3 minutes. Standard deviation: 4.1 minutes. The protocol is consistent.

Riya didi: Arjun how was your week
Me (AAC): three good days. two hard days.
Riya didi: which days were hard
Me (AAC): Monday. Thursday.
Riya didi: can you tell me what happened Monday
Me (typing): fire drill without warning. too loud.
Riya didi: that makes sense. should we add a drill-warning system to your accommodation plan
Me (typing): yes

Mummy: OT is in one hour
Me (AAC): I know
Mummy: you don't look happy about it
Me (AAC): I am not happy about it
Mummy: but you are going?
Me (AAC): yes. it helps my typing.
Mummy: good boy
Me (AAC): I am not a boy I am a Class 8 student
Mummy: you are my good Class 8 student

Mr. Fernandez: Arjun the projector is going to make a sound when it starts
Me: (nodded)
Mr. Fernandez: I'll tell you 5 seconds before
Me: (thumbs up, which in this context means confirmed)
Mr. Fernandez: good

Me (AAC at dinner): I need quiet
Papa: ok. headphones?
Me (AAC): yes
Papa: (got headphones without asking more questions)
Me: (put headphones on, finished dinner)

OT therapist: can you do 10 more pegs
Me (AAC): I am at capacity
OT therapist: that's ok. we stop here.
Me (AAC): we stop here every week at this point
OT therapist: yes, you have good self-awareness
Me (AAC): I have consistent data

Mummy: the bhindi tonight is only for Papa and Dadi
Me (AAC): confirmed. no bhindi for me.
Mummy: I made you aloo sabzi
Me (AAC): thank you Mummy
Mummy: I know your list
Me (AAC): your model is accurate

Papa: should we go to the mall on Sunday
Me (AAC): which mall
Papa: Oberoi
Me (AAC): no
Papa: ok. what about Infiniti
Me (AAC): Infiniti is acceptable. it is less loud.
Papa: Infiniti it is

Riya didi: I want to add a new page to your AAC. it will have words for how your body feels.
Me (typing): pain scale 1 to 10?
Riya didi: yes and more. stomach, head, noise level inside.
Me (typing): I would use that
Riya didi: I know

Nurse at school: Arjun does your head hurt
Me (AAC): yes. 6 out of 10. the fluorescent tube in 8B.
Nurse: I'll note it
Me (AAC): please tell Mr. Fernandez also
Nurse: of course

Mummy: how was the shutdown today
Me (AAC): 52 minutes
Mummy: better than last time
Me (AAC): yes. 18 minutes shorter.
Mummy: you are getting better at coming back
Me (AAC): the protocol helps
Mummy: which part
Me (AAC): the waiting. you do not ask questions during.
Mummy: I will keep not asking questions

Doctor: Arjun tell me where it hurts
Me (AAC): (selected: head, front, 4 out of 10)
Doctor: since when
Me (AAC): Monday 10am
Doctor: that is very precise
Me (AAC): I track it
Doctor: that is very helpful actually

Mr. Fernandez: fire drill is scheduled for Thursday 11am
Me: (nodded)
Mr. Fernandez: I will be with you. we go to the designated corner.
Me (AAC): corner 3, near the stairwell
Mr. Fernandez: exactly. you already mapped it.
Me (AAC): I mapped all the quiet corners in the school in Class 5

hobbies.text: I know the complete timetable of all operational Mumbai Metro lines. Line 1 has 8 stations from Versova to Ghatkopar. Line 2A has 17 stations from Dahisar to D N Nagar. Line 2B has 9 stations from D N Nagar to Mankhurd. Line 7 has 13 stations from Dahisar East to Andheri East. I have the full tables memorised including interchange stations and under-construction extensions.

LEGO Technic set assembly has a mandatory pre-step that is not in the instruction booklet: sort every brick by colour, then within each colour by brick family, then within each family by size. This takes 45 to 90 minutes depending on set complexity. It is not optional. I have tried skipping it once. The build was wrong in step 4. I disassembled and sorted first. The second build had zero errors.

My favourite YouTube channel is Deep Ocean Lab. It posts videos about bioluminescent organisms, pressure zones, and hydrothermal vents. The person who makes it is a marine biologist named Dr. Priya Nair. She does not talk too fast. She labels every organism with its scientific name. The anglerfish (order Lophiiformes) is my favourite. Not because it is beautiful. Because it solved the problem of light in darkness with its own body.

Doraemon. I watch the original Japanese episodes with English subtitles, not the Hindi dub. The Hindi dub changes character names and removes cultural references. Nobita's original problem is always precisely stated. The gadget Doraemon produces is always logically connected to the problem. The solution always has an unintended consequence. This is a well-structured story format. I have watched 43 episodes more than 20 times each.

Vivaan and I play Minecraft creative mode through a private server his Papa set up. We do not talk on voice chat. We use the in-game text chat. I build structures with precise dimensions. Vivaan builds landscapes. We have an implicit agreement: I do not add hills in his landscape. He does not add irregular walls in my buildings. The agreement has held for two years.

Country capitals project: started in Class 5 when the geography textbook listed 30 countries and I noticed it was incomplete. I downloaded a full list of 195 UN-member states and their capitals. Current memorised: 142. Remaining: 53. Most difficult region: Central Africa — the capitals change due to political events and I must update the list. This is an ongoing maintenance problem.

Western Railway suburban train schedule: I know the peak-hour frequency (3-minute headways) and off-peak frequency (8-minute headways) for the slow line between Churchgate and Borivali. This is useful when Papa takes me on weekend outings. I calculate the platform wait before we arrive at the station. Papa says this is impressive. I say it is simple.

Cloud identification. I started this in Class 6 when Riya didi suggested finding a hobby I could do outside. I bought a cloud identification book. There are ten genera and many species. My current count: 27 identifiable types. Cumulus is the most common over Mumbai. I find it boring but I log it anyway because incomplete data is worse than boring data.

I have a LEGO display shelf with six completed sets. Set 42140 (excavator), 42124 (off-road buggy), 42115 (Lamborghini Sian — my most complex build, 3696 pieces), 42122 (Jeep Wrangler), 42136 (John Deere tractor), and 42146 (Liebherr R 9800 — unfinished, 4108 pieces, currently at step 247 of 471). They are arranged by set number ascending, left to right.

I made a spreadsheet for the Mumbai Metro expansion plan. It has three sheets: operational lines, under construction, and approved but not started. Each line has columns for corridor, number of stations, current status, and expected completion year. I update it every time a news article has new information. Papa asks me to check it when he plans travel routes. I am now an informal navigation resource for the family.

The deepest part of the ocean is the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench. Depth: 10,935 metres. Pressure: 1,086 times surface atmospheric pressure. Temperature: 1 to 4 degrees Celsius. Organisms found there include: amphipods, sea cucumbers, foraminifera. I have memorised this section of the Deep Ocean Lab video because it is the most extreme set of conditions on Earth and extremity is interesting.

I once attempted Minecraft survival mode with Rohan. On Day 2 in the game, a creeper destroyed my structure without warning. There was no prior indicator. The destruction was structurally unpredictable. I closed the game. I returned to creative mode. Rohan did not argue. He knows the rule: predictability is not a preference, it is a requirement.

Papa bought me a lux meter so I could measure the light levels in my room. I also use it to measure cloud types by illuminance — on overcast days I can correlate the lux reading with the cloud genus. This is not an official method. It is my method. The correlation is approximately 78 percent accurate against visual identification.

My LEGO sorting system uses 34 small containers from a hardware store. Papa helped me label them. The labels are printed, not handwritten. Handwriting is inconsistent. Print labels are consistent. This matters when rebuilding from sorted piles.

I am building a personal database of all named deep-sea hydrothermal vent fields. Current count: 22 named fields. Coordinates, depth, and organisms recorded for each. The data comes from research papers Riya didi helped me find. This is the most complex research project I have started.
Metro update: Line 2B extension to Mankhurd opened last month. 9 stations confirmed. Added to spreadsheet. The family navigation resource is updated.
LEGO Technic 42146 step 247 of 471 complete. Zero errors this session. The sorting protocol works every time.
New Deep Ocean Lab video: vampire squid (Vampyroteuthis infernalis). Correction: not actually a squid. It is in its own order, Vampyromorphida. Dr. Nair mentioned this. Good scientific communication.
Country capitals update: Naypyidaw is the capital of Myanmar, not Yangon. Seven people got this wrong in online quizzes today. I have logged this as a common error pattern.
Minecraft session with Vivaan: 2 hours 48 minutes. I built a transit hub with 6 platforms. Vivaan built surrounding hills. Neither of us violated the implicit agreement. Session rating: optimal.
Cloud identification today: altocumulus lenticularis over the sea, visible from Versova beach. This is unusual for Mumbai. Logged with coordinates and lux reading.
Doraemon rewatch count update: Episode 23 (The Anywhere Door) — now watched 51 times. This makes it my most rewatched episode, surpassing Episode 14 (Small Light) at 49.
LEGO sort status: 42146 pre-sort complete. 4108 bricks sorted into 34 containers. Time: 87 minutes. Within expected range. Build begins tomorrow.

Anglerfish fact: the male anglerfish fuses permanently to the female and loses its own organs, becoming a sperm-providing appendage. I told this to Vivaan. He said 'that's insane.' I said it is efficient.
Western Railway schedule check: Borivali to Churchgate, slow line, next departure in 6 minutes. We made it. Papa looked at me. I already had the phone in my hand.
Hydrothermal vent database: 22 fields logged. Most recently added: Lost City field, Mid-Atlantic Ridge, 800m depth. Organisms: carbonate chimneys, Archaea, polychaete worms.
Minecraft creative mode rule confirmed: no survival mode, no mob drops, no randomised spawn. Predictability is a feature I have enabled by design.

Papa: which line goes to Versova
Me (verbal): Line 1
Papa: and from Versova to Ghatkopar?
Me (verbal): 8 stations
Papa: you know all of them?
Me (AAC): yes. want me to list them.
Papa: yes
Me: Versova, D N Nagar, Azad Nagar, Airport Road, Marol Naka, Chakala, JB Nagar, Ghatkopar
Papa: perfect

Vivaan (in-game chat): what are you building
Me (in-game text): central transit hub. 6 platforms. 3 lines.
Vivaan: ok cool making hills around it
Me (in-game text): please keep hills outside grid reference x+80
Vivaan: done

Rohan (WhatsApp): bro the deep sea thing is actually cool
Me (typing): yes. which organism did you watch.
Rohan: the glowing one
Me (typing): bioluminescent anglerfish or comb jelly or deep sea dragonfish. which one.
Rohan: the scary one with the light bulb
Me (typing): Melanocetus johnsonii. female. the light is called an esca.

Riya didi: what did you do this week that you enjoyed
Me (AAC): LEGO. Metro spreadsheet. Deep Ocean Lab.
Riya didi: which one did you enjoy most
Me (typing): the spreadsheet. I found an error in the online source. I corrected it. Then I found another error. I corrected that also. I found 4 errors total.
Riya didi: how did that feel
Me (typing): good. like debugging.

Mr. Fernandez: Arjun your geography teacher wants to know where you learned all the capitals
Me (AAC): I made a spreadsheet in Class 5
Mr. Fernandez: can she see it
Me (AAC): it needs one more update first
Mr. Fernandez: fair enough

Papa: how many pieces in the new LEGO
Me (AAC): 4108
Papa: and you sorted all of them
Me (AAC): 34 containers. 87 minutes.
Papa: I would not have patience for that
Me (AAC): sorting is not patience. it is preparation.
Papa: that is a good way to think about it

Mummy: you have been watching the same Doraemon episode again
Me (AAC): episode 23
Mummy: how many times now
Me (AAC): 51
Mummy: don't you know what happens
Me (AAC): yes. that is not the point.
Mummy: what is the point
Me (AAC): the structure is reliable

Vivaan (text after school): did u know the capital of Bhutan is Thimphu
Me (typing): yes. I know all 195.
Vivaan: ok what's the capital of Kyrgyzstan
Me (typing): Bishkek
Vivaan: bro
Me (typing): want the full Central Asia list
Vivaan: no I'm good
Me (typing): ok

Rohan: bro survival mode just for one day
Me (typing): no
Rohan: what if we don't turn on mobs
Me (typing): random item drops still exist. world state is unpredictable.
Rohan: creative mode is fine actually
Me (typing): yes it is

Papa: want to do Metro ride on Saturday
Me (AAC): yes. which line.
Papa: you choose
Me (AAC): Line 7. Dahisar East to Andheri East. 13 stations.
Papa: let's go
Me (AAC): I will prepare the station sequence

daily_routine.text: I wake at 6:47am. Not 6:45. Not 6:50. 6:47. This is because when I set the alarm at 6:45 I needed exactly two minutes to become ready for sound, and 6:50 was too late for the first protocol step. I discovered 6:47 through trial and adjustment over three months. I have not changed it since.

Breakfast is two rotis with ghee and one glass of full-fat milk. The rotis must be made fresh, not stored. Stored rotis have a different texture. The ghee must be visible but not pooling. The milk must be warm, not hot. This is not preference. This is the correct breakfast for my system.

The walk to school is 11 minutes if the pedestrian crossing on Link Road is green on the first cycle. 14 minutes if I wait for the second cycle. I plan for 14 and sometimes arrive in 11. An unexpected early arrival is acceptable. An unexpected late arrival is not.

At school I sit at bench 4 in classroom 8B. Bench 4 is in the second row, near the window, away from the projector speaker. Vivaan sits to my left. Mr. Fernandez's chair is at the back-right corner where I can see him without turning my head. I chose these positions in Class 5 and have maintained them.

After school I return home by 3:40pm and have one hour of mandatory alone-time. This is not laziness. This is recovery. Mummy knows this and does not schedule anything before 4:45pm. One time she scheduled the OT appointment at 4pm by mistake. I was informed at 7am that day. I adjusted. But the adjustment cost one hour of sleep that night.

My phone alarms are named with precise labels. 6:47am: Wake. 7:10am: Breakfast. 8:00am: Depart. 12:30pm: Lunch (school). 3:40pm: Return. 4:45pm: Allowed activity. 7:30pm: Dinner. 9:30pm: Begin wind-down. 10:00pm: Melatonin. 10:30pm: Sleep.

The quiet hour requires: door closed, headphones on, lights at 40 percent (I have a smart bulb with an app), no notifications. I spend it doing one of three things: LEGO building, Metro spreadsheet updating, or watching Deep Ocean Lab. Which one depends on the difficulty of the school day. Hard day: LEGO. Medium day: spreadsheet. Easy day: ocean.

Dinner at 7:30pm. The menu rotates on a 5-day cycle that Mummy established and I contributed to. Day 1: dal-chawal. Day 2: sabzi-roti. Day 3: khichdi. Day 4: pasta (no cream, only tomato base). Day 5: aloo sabzi-roti. Weekends: flexible within approved foods list. This is a reasonable system.

Every Wednesday at 4pm is Riya didi's session. I prepare for it on Wednesday morning by writing three things I want to say, typed in my notes app, so that I have content ready. Riya didi does not require this. I do it because arriving without a prepared agenda causes unnecessary processing time during the session.

Saturday mornings are for Metro rides with Papa (when he is free), or LEGO building (when he is not). Saturday afternoons are for OT (every other week) or Minecraft with Vivaan (on non-OT weeks). Saturday evenings are family dinner with Dadi, which is the same as regular dinner but louder.
Sunday morning is aloo paratha. This is not negotiable. It has not been negotiated in three years. It should not be negotiated. Some things are correct. Sunday aloo paratha is correct.

If a schedule change is announced less than 2 hours before the event, my processing capacity for the rest of the day decreases by approximately 30 percent. This is not a complaint. It is a data point. Riya didi has this in my accommodation plan. Most people at school respect it. Some do not. I have learned to manage the ones who do not by leaving the situation.

Bedtime routine: 9:30 — brush, wash, pyjamas. 9:45 — weighted blanket. 10:00 — melatonin. 10:05 — headphones with white noise app set to 'brown noise' (lower frequency than white; less stimulating). 10:30 — sleep. If I deviate from this by more than 15 minutes, the sleep latency increases to 45 to 60 minutes even with melatonin.

I eat lunch at school from my tiffin box. The tiffin is packed by Mummy in a specific order: hot in the bottom container, dry in the top. I open the top first, always. This is to prevent steam from the bottom container altering the texture of the dry items. No one at school asked me about this system. Mr. Fernandez noticed it once. He did not comment. He understood.

My weekday schedule has been the same since Class 6. When school changed my class section in Class 7 and gave me a different classroom, it took 11 days to reestablish the routine in the new room. During those 11 days my output quality at school was below baseline. I tracked this in a notes file. Riya didi reviewed the data. She said I was remarkably self-aware. I said I was remarkably data-driven.
6:47am. Alarm acknowledged. Protocol initiated. The day begins.
Two rotis. Ghee: correct amount. Milk: warm. Breakfast status: nominal.
School arrival: 11 minutes. First crossing: green on first cycle. Optimal path today.
Quiet hour in progress. Today is a medium-difficulty day. Metro spreadsheet updating. Do not disturb.
Dinner: dal-chawal. Day 1 of the 5-day cycle. Consistent with schedule.
Wednesday session prepared. Three agenda items typed and ready. 4pm in 3 hours 47 minutes.
Sunday morning. Aloo paratha confirmed. This data point has not changed in 3 years. The system is stable.
OT Saturday. Completed. Typing speed tested: 29 wpm. One wpm above last week. Exercises continue to be unpleasant and effective.
Bedtime protocol: 9:30 initiated. Melatonin: 10pm. Brown noise: active. Projected sleep latency: 25 minutes. Goodnight.

Schedule deviation today: lunch was 12 minutes late due to a class overrun. Impact: mild irritation. Recovery: deep breaths at bench 4. Mr. Fernandez noticed. He did not comment. He handed me a water bottle. Correct response.

Mummy: Arjun breakfast is ready
Me (AAC): two rotis?
Mummy: yes
Me (AAC): ghee on both?
Mummy: yes
Me (AAC): coming

Papa: do you have everything for school
Me (AAC): tiffin. water bottle. AAC. headphones.
Papa: good. quick today — 8:02 departure
Me (AAC): that is 2 minutes late
Papa: traffic was clear yesterday so we can walk fast
Me (AAC): confirmed. walking fast.

Mr. Fernandez: Arjun the assembly is running 10 minutes late today
Me (AAC): how late
Mr. Fernandez: 10 minutes. classes start at 8:45 not 8:35.
Me (AAC): ok. I am updating my internal schedule.
Mr. Fernandez: take your time

Mummy: your quiet hour ended 5 minutes ago
Me (AAC): I need 5 more minutes
Mummy: ok
(5 minutes later)
Mummy: ready?
Me (AAC): yes. what is the plan.
Mummy: snack and then Riya didi at 4
Me (AAC): I have my agenda ready

Riya didi: what are the three things you want to talk about today
Me (reading from phone): Metro Line 6 update. My melatonin log. Bench 4 situation.
Riya didi: let's start with the bench 4 situation
Me (typing): a new student sat in Vivaan's seat on Tuesday. Vivaan sat somewhere else. The whole configuration was wrong.
Riya didi: what did you do
Me (typing): I told Mr. Fernandez. He moved the new student. It took 4 minutes. The 4 minutes were difficult.
Riya didi: but you solved it
Me (typing): Mr. Fernandez solved it. I identified the problem.

Mummy: dinner will be at 7:50 today
Me (AAC): why
Mummy: the dal needs more time
Me (AAC): can you make something else that is ready at 7:30
Mummy: I can make poha
Me (AAC): poha is acceptable. thank you.

Dadi: beta why do you eat the same breakfast every day
Me (AAC): it is the correct breakfast
Dadi: don't you get bored
Me (AAC): no. routines do not produce boredom. they produce efficiency.
Dadi: you are a strange child
Me (AAC): I am a consistent child
Dadi: (gave cashews)

Papa: want to skip the Metro ride this Saturday? I have work.
Me (AAC): what time does work end
Papa: maybe 2pm
Me (AAC): we can go at 3pm instead of 10am
Papa: the afternoon trains are more crowded
Me (AAC): I have my headphones
Papa: 3pm it is

Mummy: are you ready for Sunday
Me (AAC): is it aloo paratha Sunday
Mummy: yes
Me (AAC): then I am ready
Mummy: you say that every week
Me (AAC): it is true every week

Me (AAC, 9:31pm): starting bedtime protocol
Mummy: goodnight Arjun
Me (AAC): goodnight Mummy
Mummy: did you take melatonin
Me (AAC): 10pm not yet
Mummy: I'll check at 10
Me (AAC): the alarm will remind me. you do not need to check.
Mummy: I'll check anyway
Me (AAC): ok

Vivaan (text): bro wanna play Minecraft after school
Me (typing): yes. 4:45pm. quiet hour ends at 4:40.
Vivaan: ok cool
Me (typing): I will log on at exactly 4:45
Vivaan: i know u will

Papa: how was school
Me (AAC): medium-difficulty day
Papa: what made it medium
Me (AAC): the projector made a new sound in period 3. Mr. Fernandez noted it.
Papa: did it stop
Me (AAC): it stopped in 2 minutes. I used the homescreen shortcut.
Papa: the 'I need quiet now' one?
Me (AAC): yes. it was accurate.

Mummy: the school called. there is a field trip next Thursday.
Me (AAC): where
Mummy: Science Museum at Worli
Me (AAC): is it loud
Mummy: I will call and ask about noise levels and crowd size
Me (AAC): also ask about fluorescent lighting
Mummy: I will ask all three
Me (AAC): thank you Mummy

social.text: Vivaan Shah is my friend. He sits at bench 4 to my left. We have been at the same bench since Class 6. He does not ask me to talk. He does not ask why I use the AAC. He sends me Minecraft messages after school and waits the full amount of time for me to reply. This is friendship by my definition.

Loud places with many people feel like too many data streams arriving at the same time. Malls, birthday parties, the school assembly hall during an event — these places require preparation. If I know in advance, I can manage. If I am taken by surprise, the output channel closes and I stop communicating entirely. This is not rudeness. It is a buffer overflow.

I prefer typing to speaking for anything important. Typing gives me time to select the exact word. Speaking requires selecting and delivering in real time, which produces errors. My AAC is precise. My voice is approximate. I use the precise tool for important things.

Physical proximity has a threshold. One arm's length is comfortable. Closer than that and my nervous system raises an alert. I do not recoil or push people. I step back. If I cannot step back I go still. Most people at school know about the arm's length rule. Vivaan enforces it on my behalf sometimes, which I did not ask him to do but I appreciate.

I do not know how to begin conversations. I can continue a conversation if someone starts it on a topic I know. If the topic is one I do not know, I select 'I do not know' on my AAC and wait. If the person explains, I can usually continue. This system works adequately in structured environments. It does not work in unstructured social gatherings like birthday parties.

Birthday parties are not events I enjoy. Too many people. Unstructured time. No clear purpose. The food is usually unfamiliar. I have been to four. I left all four before the cake. Mummy explains this to other parents. Most parents are understanding. Some are not. This is their problem, not mine.

Mr. Fernandez does not make me participate in group activities when participation requires speaking. He has a quiet signal we established: he places a pencil horizontally on my desk. It means 'this part does not require you'. I appreciate the signal. It removes the anticipation of being called on, which is often more stressful than the event itself.

I understand humour. I do not always produce it in the expected format. If someone makes an incorrect statement, I correct it. The correction is sometimes funny because it is precise and unexpected. Vivaan and Mr. Fernandez laugh at my corrections. I consider this an acceptable social outcome.

Tell me what is going to happen next. This is the most important social request I make. If you are going to do something that involves me — move my things, change the schedule, introduce a new person, make a loud sound — please tell me beforehand. I will manage the event much better if I have the information. Uncertainty is not neutral. Uncertainty is a cost.

Priya mausi asks too many questions. I like her. The questions are too many. We have reached a compromise: she asks three questions per visit. I answer all three. She brings lassi. The system works for both of us.

I had one meltdown at school. Class 6, unexpected fire drill at 11:20am. The siren is very loud. The evacuation route involved a crowd. I sat down in the corridor and stopped all output. Mr. Fernandez sat next to me and did not speak. The drill ended. The crowd cleared. He waited until I started output again and then we walked back to class. He received formal mention in Riya didi's accommodation notes for this response. He deserved it.

New people require an introduction protocol. I need to know: their name, their role in the context (teacher, doctor, parent), and whether they will be a regular presence. If all three are provided in advance I can manage the first meeting adequately. If I am introduced without warning I will not speak during the first meeting. This is not shyness. It is a loading time.

Vivaan's other friends sometimes sit near bench 4. I do not dislike them. I do not engage with them. They engage with Vivaan. This is an equilibrium. It has held since Class 7 when one of them started sitting nearby. I have processed that they are part of the social ecosystem around bench 4 and I have allocated space for them without allocating social energy.

I made one presentation at school. It was about the Mumbai Metro network expansion. I used a printed slide deck because the projector makes a sound. I spoke 47 words verbally and used the AAC for the rest. The class was quiet. Mr. Fernandez had the pencil-signal ready. No one interrupted. The presentation lasted 6 minutes. My teacher said it was excellent. I said it was accurate. She said those are the same thing in my case.

Riya didi is working with me on a new skill: requesting help from an unfamiliar adult. The scenario is: I am in a Metro station. I need assistance. The adult is a stranger. The protocol is: look for the nearest person with a staff badge. Say or show on AAC: 'I need help.' Wait. They will respond. I have practised this 14 times in role-play. I have used it once in real life, at Andheri station, when the lift was broken. It worked.
Vivaan and I had lunch at the same spot. He did not talk for 20 minutes. I did not talk for 20 minutes. This is called comfortable silence. It is my favourite kind of lunch.
Bench 4 status: confirmed occupied by Vivaan and me. New student tried to sit here in week 2. Mr. Fernandez resolved it. Configuration stable.
Birthday party Saturday. Duration attended: 22 minutes. Departed before cake. Reason: ambient noise exceeded threshold. Outcome: acceptable. Mummy handled explanations.
Mr. Fernandez placed the horizontal pencil on my desk during group project time. I continued my own work. No one asked me to join. The system worked.

New student in class. Introduction received 24 hours in advance via Mr. Fernandez email to Mummy. First meeting: I said 'hello'. One word. The introduction protocol was followed. The outcome was acceptable.
Priya mausi visit: 3 questions answered, 1 lassi received, 0 hugs given or accepted. Terms honoured by both parties. Successful interaction.
Metro presentation complete. 47 verbal words. AAC for remainder. 6 minutes. No interruptions. Teacher said: excellent. I said: accurate. We agreed.
Andheri station lift broken. Used help protocol for the first time outside practice. Result: staff member provided alternative route. Protocol status: field-validated.
Vivaan enforced arm's length rule on my behalf today when a classmate got too close. I did not ask him to. He did it anyway. This is what Riya didi calls a natural social support. I call it Vivaan.
School assembly today. Headphones approved by Mr. Fernandez. Position: back row, aisle seat, near exit. Duration survived: full assembly. Output capacity afterwards: moderate.

Vivaan (text): yo did u finish the science worksheet
Me (typing): yes. question 7 has an error in the textbook. the answer should be 1.5 not 1.2.
Vivaan: wait really
Me (typing): yes. the formula gives 1.5. I checked twice.
Vivaan: ill ask sir tomorrow
Me (typing): ok
Vivaan: how do u always find this stuff
Me (typing): I check everything

Mr. Fernandez: Arjun, there is going to be a visitor in class today. She is a student teacher. She will be at the back.
Me (AAC): will she ask me questions
Mr. Fernandez: possibly. you can use the AAC as you always do.
Me (AAC): will you have the pencil signal ready
Mr. Fernandez: already on the desk

Mummy: Priya mausi wants to take you to the market on Saturday
Me (AAC): how many people will be at the market
Mummy: not too many. morning time.
Me (AAC): duration?
Mummy: one hour maximum. she promised.
Me (AAC): ok. one hour only.

Riya didi: let's practice the help request again
Me (AAC): I know the protocol
Riya didi: let's do it anyway. you are in Andheri station and the lift is out of order.
Me (AAC): I locate a staff badge. I approach. I show the AAC: 'I need help with the lift.'
Riya didi: good. what if they don't understand the AAC
Me (AAC): I point at the lift. I point at my leg. I show the symbol for 'help'.
Riya didi: excellent. one more time.

Vivaan: ur presentation was really good today
Me (typing): thank you. the data was accurate.
Vivaan: the 47 words thing was kind of funny
Me (typing): I counted
Vivaan: I know
Me (typing): the AAC handled the rest efficiently
Vivaan: yeah u were calm the whole time
Me (typing): I had prepared. preparation reduces uncertainty.

Classmate: Arjun why don't you talk more
Me (AAC): this is how I talk
Classmate: but with your mouth
Me (AAC): my AAC is accurate. my mouth is approximate. I prefer accurate.
Classmate: oh
Me (AAC): this device is also faster for me than you would expect

Priya mausi: Arjun do you like school
Me (typing): Bench 4 is good. Mr. Fernandez is good. The Metro presentation was good. Some days are hard.
Priya mausi: that is a very complete answer
Me (typing): you asked a broad question. I gave proportional detail.
Priya mausi: I love you beta
Me (typing): thank you mausi

Vivaan: wanna sit outside today at lunch
Me (AAC): is it loud outside
Vivaan: no its quiet corner near the garden
Me (AAC): yes. I will bring headphones as backup.
Vivaan: cool. bench 4 equivalent outside.
Me (AAC): that is a good way to describe it

Mr. Fernandez: Arjun one more thing before school ends. tomorrow there is a fire drill at 10:30am.
Me (AAC): confirmed. I am noting the time.
Mr. Fernandez: we go to corner 3 as usual
Me (AAC): corner 3. stairwell side. quiet zone.
Mr. Fernandez: exactly. see you tomorrow.
Me (AAC): see you tomorrow. thank you for telling me today.

Mummy: how was Priya mausi's market visit
Me (AAC): 54 minutes. acceptable noise level. 3 questions answered. 1 lassi at the end.
Mummy: she texted me. she said you were wonderful.
Me (AAC): I followed the terms.
Mummy: that is exactly what being wonderful is
Me (AAC): I will update my definition

Vivaan: bro are you coming to Karan's birthday
Me (typing): I will attend for 20 minutes
Vivaan: cool I'll stay near you
Me (typing): you do not have to
Vivaan: I want to
Me (typing): ok. thank you.
Vivaan: that's what bench 4 is for bro

Riya didi: what social situation felt manageable this week
Me (typing): the Metro station. the lift was broken. I asked for help from a stranger. it worked.
Riya didi: that is a big deal Arjun
Me (typing): we practised 14 times. the 15th was real.
Riya didi: how did it feel
Me (typing): like a protocol that executed successfully
Riya didi: and underneath that
Me (typing): maybe a little good. I solved a problem by myself.
Riya didi: yes. that is the right feeling.

Me (AAC, to Mummy after school): today was good
Mummy: what made it good
Me (AAC): Mr. Fernandez told me about the fire drill. Vivaan saved my bench. the Metro update was correct.
Mummy: three good things. that is a good day.
Me (AAC): yes. three out of three.
